title: SEGMENT 1 — Why the same drug does different things to different people

lines.text: SEGMENT 1 — Why the same drug does different things to different people
Alright, let's get started.
Good morning, everyone.
For the next two hours we're going to talk about one of the most clinically immediate fields in all of modern genetics — pharmacogenomics, the science of how your DNA shapes your response to drugs.
And before I tell you a single mechanism, I want to plant a scene in your head that I want you to carry through the whole lecture.
Imagine three patients.
They're all the same age.
They're all roughly the same weight.

They all have the same diagnosis, let's say a blood clot in the leg, and their doctor prescribes them all the same dose of the same drug — warfarin, a blood thinner that's been on the market since the 1950s and is still one of the most prescribed anticoagulants in the world.
Same drug.
Same dose.
Same indication.
Three patients.
Patient one takes their pill every day for a month and shows up to the clinic with their blood beautifully thinned, exactly in the therapeutic range, clot dissolving, doctor happy.
Patient two takes the exact same pill, every day for a month, and shows up to the clinic with blood that's barely thinned at all.
The clot hasn't moved.
The drug is, in their body, essentially doing nothing.
Their doctor doubles the dose.
Now they're in range.

Patient three takes the exact same pill, and after a week they show up in the emergency room bleeding from their gums, bruising spontaneously, with blood that's so thin it won't clot if they nick themselves shaving.
Their dose, for them, was a near-fatal overdose.
If they hadn't gotten lucky and noticed, they could have bled into their brain and died.
Same drug.
Same dose.
Same patient profile on paper.
Three radically different outcomes.
This isn't a hypothetical.
This is what warfarin clinics looked like for sixty years, and to a significant extent still do.

The drug has a fivefold range of doses across the population, and figuring out which patient needs which dose has historically been done by trial and error, with bleeding events and recurrent clots as the way you learn.
Now here's the question that pharmacogenomics asks.
Why?
Why do three people, given the same chemical, experience such different outcomes?
What is different about them, biologically, that produces these different responses?
There are several answers, and we'll go through them.
Some of the variation is environmental — what you eat, what other drugs you're on, how much you drink.
Some of it is physiological — your age, your weight, your kidney function, your liver function.
Some of it is disease-related — a patient with congestive heart failure metabolizes drugs differently than a patient without.
But a very large, often dominant, chunk of the variation has nothing to do with any of that.
It has to do with the DNA you inherited from your parents.
Specifically, it has to do with single-letter differences in a handful of genes — genes that code for the enzymes that activate, deactivate, and clear drugs from your body.
Change one letter in one of those genes, and warfarin can be life-saving at one milligram per day or a near-fatal overdose at the same dose.
That insight — that drug response is, in large part, a genetic trait — is the founding insight of pharmacogenomics.
And once you accept it, the clinical implication is enormous.
If we could read those genes before we ever prescribed the drug, we could match the dose, or even the choice of drug, to the patient's actual metabolic biology.
We could replace trial and error with a lookup table.

We could stop killing people with morphine toxicity, stop letting clots throw to people's brains because their drug isn't working, stop giving children with leukemia bone-marrow-killing doses of a perfectly normal medicine.
That's the promise.
And for about thirty drug-gene pairs, we now actually deliver on it routinely.

If you walk out of here in two hours and you can do three things — one, explain to a friend without jargon why the same drug can have wildly different effects on different people, and roughly which enzymes are doing the varying; two, name a few of the canonical drug-gene pairs and what genotype predicts what outcome; three, look at a news headline about a "personalized medicine breakthrough" and understand whether what's being described is real, useful, and implementable, or whether it's
somebody selling vapor — then we've succeeded.
Everything else is detail hung onto that scaffold.
The single-sentence definition I want you to carry through the whole lecture is this.
Pharmacogenomics is the genetics of drug response — same dose, different person, different outcome, often because of a single variant in a single enzyme.
Let's build the scaffold.
